win_rule.headline: Winning Kenya's presidency takes more than a national majority
conditions: More than 50% of all valid votes cast nationwide (50% + 1).
At least 25% of the votes cast in a minimum of 24 of Kenya's 47 counties.
win_rule.if_no_winner: If no candidate meets both conditions, a run-off between the top two is held within 30 days.
win_rule.why_it_matters: A candidate can lead the national vote and still fail the county-distribution test, or be forced into a run-off. This is why coalition breadth across regions, not just a high headline number, decides the outcome — and why a fragmented field tends to favour whoever has the widest regional base.

calendar.election_date_note: Kenya holds general elections on the second Tuesday of August every five years; the 2027 date is fixed by that constitutional schedule.

registration.registered_voters_2022_note: Approximately 22.1 million Kenyans were registered for the August 2022 general election.
registration.expected_new_voters_note: An estimated 5.7 million new voters — mostly young Kenyans — are expected to register ahead of 2027, a bloc large enough to reshape the electorate. Youth turnout is historically lower and harder to poll, making this the single biggest source of uncertainty in any 2027 projection.

historical_results.note: Kenyatta won a narrow first-round majority; Odinga contested at the Supreme Court, which upheld the result.

August 2017 result (Kenyatta 54.2% / Odinga 44.7%) was annulled by the Supreme Court over process failures. A fresh October 2017 election, boycotted by Odinga, returned Kenyatta with 98% on ~39% turnout.
One of the closest presidential results in Kenyan history; upheld by the Supreme Court after a challenge. Turnout was notably lower than 2017.

zones.note: Kenyan pollsters typically report against 8–9 analytical zones grouping counties with broadly similar voting patterns. These are used here only as a reference framework for reading regional context — this project holds no invented per-zone vote data.
